message.content: FIX-REGRESSION AUDIT (#pass-7). Read the diff of the immediately-prior pass (pass 6) at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay: git commits touching scripts/capture_transcripts.py (CV-O-1, scrub-all-text-files), src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py (CV-M-1, stability_threshold None sentinel), src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py (CV-S-1, _int_env). Every fix is NEW code — does any introduce a follow-on defect (the #H-001→#CV-M-1 class)? E.g.: does scrubbing ALL text files now corrupt a file that must stay verbatim, or change a hash/manifest, or mangle JSON the engine later re-reads? Does the None-sentinel stability_threshold break any caller passing the old 0.9 default, or the digest/exploratory path? Does _int_env change import behavior? Be concrete; cite file:line. Return JSON findings: id (P7-<dim>-N), severity, file, line, title, description, poc, suggested_fix. ONLY genuine reproducible defects — do not manufacture nits.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

    fix(hardening-p6): 3 fresh findings, incl. a HIGH the convergence call missed (CV-O-1,M-1,S-1)
    
    Pass-6 was a convergence-verification pass: the 4 pass-5 split findings (H-005/006/019/020)
    re-verified as ALL-REJECTED (genuinely elective, not defects) — but the fresh 3-assessor sweep

    dimension.)
    
    - CV-O-1 (HIGH) transcript redaction was bypassed for every non-.jsonl file: the session
      subtree's .json workflow/subagent transcripts were copied RAW, leaking operator PII (email,

      scrubs EVERY text file (decode-strict to classify; binary copied verbatim). All five prior
      passes' scrubbing work (#F-024/#F-051/#G-007/#H-018) only ever applied to .jsonl.
    - CV-M-1 (medium) follow-on regression of #H-001: stability_threshold's confirm-time default of
      0.9 was indistinguishable from a caller passing 0.9, so a hypothesis that LOCKED a threshold
      != 0.9 always raised a spurious "contradicts pre-registered" error unless restated. The
      confirm-time default is now a None sentinel: locked-wins-else-supplied, fall back to 0.9 only
      when neither is given.
    - CV-S-1 (low) follow-on of #H-022: ASSAY_TRACING_PORT parsed via _int_env (names the bad var)
      instead of a bare import-time int().
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 535 passed, 5 skipped.

6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).

Now I understand the data flow. The capture is purely a copy-to-repo provenance sink (source → transcripts/sessions). The engine does NOT re-read these scrubbed files; they're write-only provenance. So mangling JSON the engine re-reads is not applicable here — destination is transcripts/, not engine input.

But there is a real concern with CV-O-1. Let me analyze the `.decode("utf-8")` strict change carefully. Previously, non-.jsonl files were copied as raw bytes (verbatim, lossless). Now ALL files decode strict then re-encode. There are two follow-on defects to check:

1. **Round-trip byte fidelity**: For non-.jsonl files that previously were copied verbatim, now they pass through `_scrub` and decode/encode. Even if scrub doesn't match, `text.encode("utf-8")` of a strict-decoded string is byte-identical, so that's fine.

2. **The real risk — over-redaction of legitimate content in JSON / .js / .txt workflow files.** The `_scrub` patterns include broad ones. Now let me check a genuine concern: the broad regex `re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+")`. Applied to ALL text files now including `.js` workflow scripts. But the deeper issue — let me examine whether there's a real correctness regression. The most concrete one I can find: **`raw.decode("utf-8")` strict vs the old `.jsonl` path used `errors="replace"`.**

This is a genuine behavioral change. Look at line 109: now `text = raw.decode("utf-8")` is STRICT. The OLD code for .jsonl used `errors="replace"`. So a `.jsonl` transcript that contains invalid UTF-8 (e.g. a truncated multibyte sequence, or latin-1 bytes from a tool output) now takes the `UnicodeDecodeError` branch and is **copied verbatim WITHOUT scrubbing** — the exact PII/secret leak CV-O-1 was meant to fix, but now for .jsonl files that previously WERE scrubbed (with replacement).

This is a real follow-on regression. Let me verify the old behavior precisely.

Confirmed. This is a genuine, reproducible follow-on defect (the #H-001→#CV-M-1 class the prompt asks about).

**Old behavior**: `.jsonl` files decoded with `errors="replace"` — ALWAYS scrubbed, even with malformed UTF-8 bytes (lone surrogates, latin-1, truncated multibyte). The replacement char only affected the invalid bytes; the rest of the text was still scrubbed.

**New behavior**: strict `decode("utf-8")`. A `.jsonl` (or `.json`) transcript containing even ONE invalid UTF-8 byte raises `UnicodeDecodeError` and falls into the `data = raw` branch → **copied byte-for-byte verbatim with NO scrubbing**. PII and secrets in that file leak — a regression of the very property CV-O-1 (and #F-024/#F-051/#G-007/#H-018) protect.

This is highly plausible in practice: Claude tool-result transcripts routinely embed arbitrary subprocess output (hexdumps, binary-ish stdout, non-UTF-8 file reads) inside JSONL. A single such byte flips the whole file from "scrubbed" to "raw-copied with secrets."

Let me verify reproducibility concretely.

findings.title: CV-O-1's strict decode silently REVERTS scrubbing for any transcript with one invalid UTF-8 byte — re-leaking the exact secrets/PII it was meant to redact
findings.description: CV-O-1 replaced the per-suffix gate with a decode-strict classifier: `try: text = raw.decode("utf-8")` then scrub, `except UnicodeDecodeError: data = raw` (copy verbatim). This introduces a follow-on regression of CV-O-1's own goal and of every prior scrubbing fix (#F-024/#F-051/#G-007/#H-018). PRE-fix, .jsonl files were decoded with `errors="replace"`, so they were ALWAYS scrubbed regardless of any malformed byte — only the single invalid byte became U+FFFD, the rest of the file (and all secrets/PII in it) was still redacted. POST-fix, a STRICT decode means a single invalid UTF-8 byte anywhere in a .jsonl/.json transcript raises UnicodeDecodeError and routes the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch, copying it byte-for-byte with NO scrubbing. Claude tool-result transcripts routinely embed arbitrary subprocess stdout (hexdumps, non-UTF-8/latin-1 file reads, truncated multibyte from a killed process) inside the JSONL string values, so this is a common, not exotic, condition. The net effect: the highest-severity privacy guarantee (no operator email/username/path + no credential-shaped secret in committed transcripts) now fails open precisely for the files most likely to contain raw dumped data. This is the #H-001 -> #CV-M-1 class the audit targets: the new code is the defect.

try: text=raw.decode('utf-8'); data=cap._scrub(text).encode('utf-8'); branch='scrub'

findings.suggested_fix: Decode for SCRUBBING with errors='replace' (or 'surrogatepass'/'backslashreplace') instead of strict, so a stray byte never disables redaction; use the decode only to scrub, not to classify text-vs-binary. If a genuine binary verbatim path is still wanted, gate it on a real binary signal (e.g. a NUL byte: `if b"\x00" in raw: data = raw`) rather than on UTF-8 validity, since UTF-8-invalidity != binary and is common in legitimate text transcripts. Concretely: `text = raw.decode('utf-8', errors='replace'); data = _scrub(text).encode('utf-8')` for all non-NUL files, mirroring the prior .jsonl behavior but now applied to every file. Add a regression test: a .jsonl containing a secret AND an invalid byte must still come out scrubbed.

content.text:
Pass-7 fix-regression audit complete. One genuine follow-on defect found.

**P7-O-1 (high)** — `scripts/capture_transcripts.py:108-113`. CV-O-1's switch from suffix-gating to a strict `raw.decode("utf-8")` classifier introduced a regression of its own privacy goal. The pre-fix `.jsonl` path used `errors="replace"`, so transcripts were always scrubbed even with malformed bytes. The new strict decode routes any file containing a single invalid UTF-8 byte (routine in tool-result subprocess dumps) to the verbatim/no-scrub branch, re-leaking secrets, operator email, username, and paths — the exact data #F-024/#F-051/#G-007/#H-018/CV-O-1 redact. Reproduced concretely. Fix: decode with `errors="replace"` for scrubbing and gate any binary-verbatim path on a real binary signal (NUL byte) rather than UTF-8 validity.

The other two fixes are clean:
- **CV-M-1** (confirm.py None sentinel) is correct: `_resolve_locked_param` returns `locked` when locked is set (ignoring a None supplied), else returns the supplied value, then falls back to 0.9 only when both are None. The downstream `0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0` validation still operates on a concrete float. No caller regression — old callers passing 0.9 still work, and the digest/exploratory (unlocked) path correctly defaults to 0.9.
- **CV-S-1** (tracing.py `_int_env`) is benign: same import-time raise semantics as the prior bare `int()`, only a clearer message; the `TypeError` guard is unreachable since `os.environ.get` always returns a str. No import-behavior change.
